text.title: Survey Question Quality Rules and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Rules for writing unbiased questions, maximizing response rate, and known failure modes when agents assist with survey work.

rule.statement: Each question must address exactly one concept; split any question that could be answered differently if interpreted two ways.
rule.rationale: Double-barreled questions ("speed and accuracy") produce uninterpretable data because the respondent cannot indicate which dimension drove their answer.

rule.statement: Keep total survey completion time under 5 minutes (median respondent); cut questions if the pilot exceeds this threshold.
rule.rationale: Surveys over 5 minutes see sharply elevated dropout and straight-lining (respondents select the same answer for all items to finish faster).

rule.statement: Place demographic questions at the end of the survey, not the beginning.
rule.rationale: Opening with demographics signals the survey is about the respondent rather than the product, reducing engagement with core questions.

rule.statement: Randomize answer order on attitudinal items and Likert blocks to neutralize order and anchoring bias.
rule.rationale: Fixed answer order produces systematic bias toward top or bottom options; randomization distributes this effect across respondents.

list.item: Length — shorter surveys have higher completion rates; every additional question reduces response.
Mobile-friendly format — many respondents complete on phone; fixed-width tables and non-tap-friendly controls cause abandonment.
Clear stated purpose — respondents complete surveys that explain why their input matters.
Mid-week, mid-day timing — Tuesday–Thursday, 10am–2pm local time consistently outperform other windows.

antipattern.description: Agent invents a 7-point Likert scale when a 5-point was specified.
antipattern.reason: Scale changes invalidate benchmark comparability; constrain the agent to the exact scale defined in the research plan.

antipattern.description: Agent codes open-ended responses and assigns confident themes to very short responses ("ok", emoji, single word).
antipattern.reason: LLMs hallucinate themes from sparse text; enforce a minimum word count before LLM coding and flag low-confidence items for human review.

antipattern.description: Agent computes NPS or SUS via LLM completion rather than deterministic code.
antipattern.reason: LLMs make arithmetic errors and confuse metric formulas; use the provided Python calculator for all metric computations.

antipattern.description: Agent treats high survey NPS as a signal that users will pay for the product.
antipattern.reason: Stated preferences overestimate willingness-to-pay by 2-4x; willingness-to-pay research requires separate conjoint or pricing studies, not a satisfaction survey.

list.item: Instrument review by a researcher before launch — agent-drafted questions need bias check before fielding.
Thematic-coding spot check: human reviews at least 10% of LLM-coded open-ended responses before finalizing themes.
Non-English surveys: native-speaker review required, especially for SUS which is particularly sensitive to wording.
